coredata.dc:title: Blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots from limeade
coredata.dc:creator: Suvarnaphaet, Phitsini
Tiwary, Chandra Sekhar
Wetcharungsri, Jutaphet
Porntheeraphat, Supanit
Hoonsawat, Rassmidara
Ajayan, Pulickel Madhavapanicker
Tang, I-Ming
Asanithi, Piyapong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based photoluminescent nanodot has currently been one of the promising materials for various applications. The remaining challenges are the carbon sources and the simple synthetic processes that enhance the quantum yield, photostability and biocompatibility of the nanodots. In this work, the synthesis of blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots from limeade via a single-step hydrothermal carbonization process is presented. Lime carbon nanodot (L-CnD), whose the quantum yield exceeding 50% for the 490nm emission in gram-scale amounts, has the structure of graphene core functionalized with the oxygen functional groups. The micron-sized flake of the as-prepared L-CnD powder exhibits multicolor emission depending on an excitation wavelength. The L-CnDs are demonstrated for rapidly ferric-ion (Fe3+) detection in water compared to Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions. The photoluminescence quenching of L-CnD solution under UV light is used to distinguish the Fe3+ ions from others by naked eyes as low concentration as 100μM. Additionally, L-CnDs provide exceptional photostability and biocompatibility for imaging yeast cell morphology. Changes in morphology of living yeast cells, i.e. cell shape variation, and budding, can be observed in a minute-period until more than an hour without the photoluminescent intensity loss.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blue photoluminescence
Carbon nanodots
Hydrothermal carbonization
Ferric-ion detection
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of metal-ion solutions for rapid detection
Preparation of yeast cell for morphology imaging
Physical, chemical and optical properties of L-CnDs
L-CnDs as a probe for rapid detection of ferric ions in aqueous solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the L-CnD synthesis process via HTC of limeade and photos of L-CnD powder and L-CnD solutions under daylight (left) and UV light (right); see Fig. 1S for more details. (b) HRTEM image of L-CnDs and their size distribution (inset). (c) HRTEM image of L-CnDs of different d-spacings and the FFT of the selected area diffraction.

(a) Raman spectrum of L-CnDs fitted by two-peak Gaussian function to present the D- and G-bands. (b) FTIR spectrum of L-CnDs. (c) XPS analysis of the 1s electrons of the carbon, oxygen and nitrogen, respectively. (d) EDX elemental mappings of L-CnDs: carbon (left), oxygen (middle) and nitrogen (right).

(a) UV–Vis absorption (black line) and PL (blue line) spectra of the solution of L-CnDs using PL excitation wavelength of 325nm. (b)–(d) CLSM emission images and emission spectra of the micron-sized flake of as-prepared L-CnD powder using different excitation lasers. The scale bar in (b) was 25nm.

(a) Change in the UV–Vis absorption spectra and photos of photoluminescence quenching of L-CnDs solutions in the presence of Fe3+ ion (red line), Fe2+ ion (blue line) and DI-water as a reference sample (black line). (b) Schematic illustration of the charge-transfer interactions between L-CnDs and Fe3+ ions. (c) The difference of A/A0 ratios for various metal ions rapidly detecting by L-CnDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of L-CnDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (b) Linear fit of absorbance ratio (A/A 0) at the absorption wavelength of 300nm with respect to the different concentrations. (c) Photographs of photoluminescent quenching trace of L-CnDs detecting to Fe3+ ions, i.e., 0, 0.020, 0.099, 0.196, 0.385, 0.566, 0.740 or 0.909mM, respectively. The photos were taken immediately after mixing Fe3+ ions into L-CnDs solutions.

(a) CLSM image of L-CnDs on living yeast cells. A1, A2 and A3 indicated the budding processes while D1 and D2 indicated the abnormal shape of yeast cells. (b) Change in the average intensities of L-CnDs on yeast cells as a function of the observation time (taking under laser). (c) CLSM image and three-plane section of the yeast cells with L-CnDs internalized. (d) Comparison of quantum yields of carbon nanodots from different resources and methods in literatures. The first red bar showed the quantum yield of L-CnDs in this report.

head.ce:title: Blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots from limeade

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ThEP Center, Commission of Higher Education, 328 Si Ayuthaya Rd, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon-based photoluminescent nanodot has currently been one of the promising materials for various applications. The remaining challenges are the carbon sources and the simple synthetic processes that enhance the quantum yield, photostability and biocompatibility of the nanodots. In this work, the synthesis of blue photoluminescent carbon nanodots from limeade via a single-step hydrothermal carbonization process is presented. Lime carbon nanodot (L-CnD), whose the quantum yield exceeding 50% for the 490nm emission in gram-scale amounts, has the structure of graphene core functionalized with the oxygen functional groups. The micron-sized flake of the as-prepared L-CnD powder exhibits multicolor emission depending on an excitation wavelength. The L-CnDs are demonstrated for rapidly ferric-ion (Fe3+) detection in water compared to Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions. The photoluminescence quenching of L-CnD solution under UV light is used to distinguish the Fe3+ ions from others by naked eyes as low concentration as 100μM. Additionally, L-CnDs provide exceptional photostability and biocompatibility for imaging yeast cell morphology. Changes in morphology of living yeast cells, i.e. cell shape variation, and budding, can be observed in a minute-period until more than an hour without the photoluminescent intensity loss.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of carbon-based dots have attracted much attention due to their possible applications, including optoelectronics devices [1–3], colorimetric sensor arrays [4] and biomedical applications [5,6]. As the size of these carbon-based dots reduces to the zero dimensional (0D) regimes [7], for example graphene quantum dot (GQD), carbon nanodot (CnD) and polymer dot (PD), they start showing special photoluminescence properties. The photoluminescence of the carbon-based dots do not only arise from the recombination of confined electron–hole (e–h) pairs within the conjugated π-domains of the carbon-core state, but they are also due to the degree of surface chemistry and defects [7–10]. The process may begin with carbon sources such as carbon nanotubes [11], graphene oxide [12], and carbon fiber [13], and then chemically cutting them into smaller pieces. Pan et al. [14] showed that the hydrothermal cutting of micro-sized graphene oxide sheets, whose surfaces were passivated by CO, OH and COOH groups, can produce blue-photoluminescent GQDs. Additionally, starting with small molecules containing carbon such as chemically organic materials is also possible; for example, Zhou et al. [15] built up GQDs from polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) and the photoluminescence could to be tunable. Dong et al. [16] directly pyrolyzed citric acid leading to the evolution of nanoclusters of GQDs. Various kinds of the carbon-based dots have provided a wide range of photoluminescence colors ranging from blue to yellow depending on their sizes and shapes. The optical performance can be improved by the functional groups passivated on the surface of the carbon-based dots [2,17–21]. Nie et al. [17] synthesized the carbon-based dots from chloroform (CHCl3) and diethylamine (DEA). Their multicolor emission spectra were due to the different sizes and the surface functional groups, i.e. CO and CN. Zhang et al. [20] have investigated blue, green and orange emissions by chemically passivating reaction during the synthesis. Recent studies [22–24] have reported using techniques similar to a hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) to transform natural organic precursors into the photoluminescent carbon-based dots. Sahu et al. [22] used orange juice as a precursor to obtain the fine-structural carbon nanodots with green photoluminescence of 26% quantum yield. The work suggested that the photoluminescence enhancement was obtained from the narrow sizes of carbon nanodots. Dong et al. [24] obtained the nano-sized graphene oxide – like structure synthesized from humic substances (a product of the microbial degradation of dead plant mater) and they exhibited the blue emission. The presence of nitrogen-containing groups such as NH2 and CN on the surface of the carbon-based dots can enhance their quantum yield [25–32]. Liu et al. [26] and Barati et al. [29] synthesized CnDs with a quantum yield of 37% and 40%, respectively by doping the carbonized citric acid with nitrogen from polyethyleneimine polymer and ammonium bicarbonate (NH4HCO3).

In this study, blue photoluminescent CnDs were synthesized from fresh pulp-free limeade (Citrus aurantifolia (Christm.) Swingle) using a hydrothermal carbonization process at low temperature (106°C) without any additional chemical treatment or doping. The carbon-core states of the lime carbon nanodot (L-CnD) were self-passivated by the functional groups originated from the natural acids [33,34] (citric, malic, tartaric, oxalic, niacin and ascorbic acids) and from the vitamins contained in limeade. The as-prepared L-CnDs were examined to reveal the nano-sized structure of graphene core and their surface functional groups. The L-CnD solution presented blue photoluminescence emission at 490nm in room temperature while the thin layers of L-CnD powder offered multicolor emission depending on the excitation wavelength. Two possible applications of using L-CnDs were demonstrated, including the rapid detection of ferric (Fe3+) ions and the cell morphology imaging. The CLSM quantum yield of 53.6% was achieved when the L-CnDs were internalized into the living yeast cells.

Limes were purchased from local market. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. According to the preparation of L-CnDs in Fig. 1(a), the limes were sliced into small pieces and about 125ml of fresh pure juice was squeezed out. The limeade was then centrifuged at 5000rpm for 20min to separate the lime pulps. The supernatant was filtered through a fritted glass funnel with 11-μm filter paper to obtain only pulp-free limeade. Then the limeade was transferred to a 250-ml round-bottom flask and heated at a constant temperature of 106°C using a heating mantle for ~1h. Consequently, the color of the limeade changed from being light green to being light yellow and brown, respectively. After dehydration and deformation, the dried limeade was cooled down and then 100ml of DI water was added and sonicated for 1h using an ultrasonic bath. This aqueous solution was filtered again through the fritted glass funnel with 0.22-μm cellulose nitrate membrane to obtain dark-brown sediment of dried limeade. The dark-brown powder was then annealed at 200°C for 2h in a hot air oven to obtain the dried L-CnDs (Fig. S2 for the chemical bonds in L-CnD structure observed by FTIR of L-CnD powders before/after annealing). To obtain L-CnDs solution, a few milligrams of the L-CnD powder was dissolved in DI water and sonicated for 1h. Large-sized residues were removed by filtering the L-CnD suspension through the cellulose nitrate membrane (pore size 0.22μm, Millipore Membrane). The L-CnD solution showed blue photoluminescence under UV light.

The graphitic characteristics of L-CnDs were investigated by Raman spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer) using an excitation wavelength of 532nm. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700 Spectrometer) operating in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode was used to identify the chemical functional groups. Elemental analysis was measured by XPS using a PHI Quantera X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. An aluminum cathode was used as the X-ray source. The source power was set at 48.5W, passing energies of 140.00eV for survey scans and 26.00eV for high-resolution scans. An electron dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis with element mapping was done by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) (Nova Nanosem 450 FEI field-emission scanning electron microscope) operating at 10kV. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were taken with a JEOL 2100 Field Emission Gun. Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) absorption was measured by the Thermo Scientific Orion AquaMate 8000 UV–Vis Spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL, Acton SpectraPro 2300i monochromator) was performed by the excitation wavelength of 325nm at room temperature using HeCd laser (IK Kimmon). Four excitation wavelengths of the confocal laser (Olympus FV1000), including 405nm (violet), 488nm (blue), 543nm (green) and 633nm (red), were used for scanning microimages and emission spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of metal-ion solutions for rapid detection
ce:section.ce:para: CuCl2·2H2O (Loba), CoCl2 (Sigma-Aldrich), FeCl3 ⋅6H2O (Fluka), FeCl2·3H2O (Fluka), (CH3COO)2Zn·2H2O (Univar), MnCl2·4H2O (Univar) and Ni(OCOCH3)2·4H2O (Sigma-Aldrich) were used as precursors for preparing Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ solutions, respectively. All chemicals were in analytical grade. For investigation of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions as in Fig. 4(a), L-CnDs were used for detecting Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions at a concentration of 1.67mM in DI water. For the detection of Cu2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions in Fig. 4(b), each L-CnD solution used had the same concentration of 1.67mM. The detection limit of Fe3+ ions was further investigated by varying the Fe3+ ions in DI water, including 0.020, 0.099, 0.196, 0.385, 0.566, 0.740 and 0.909mM. Two methods were used to investigate the changes in optical characteristics of L-CnD solutions in the presence of metal ions i) by naked eyes for rapid observation of photoluminescence quenching under UV lamp and ii) by UV–Vis spectroscopy for quantitative analysis of change in the absorbance intensity of L-CnDs at 300nm (n to π* transition). The UV–Vis results were interpreted by the value of A/A0 ratio, where A is absorbance of L-CnDs in the presence of metal ions and A0 is the initial absorbance of L-CnDs, i.e. without metal ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of yeast cell for morphology imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The protocols for the yeast cell preparation were as follows: i) 5.0g of sucrose was dissolved in 100ml of warm distilled water (37–45°C) and ii) 1.0g of instant yeasts was added to the sucrose solution and kept in an orbital shaker (IKA KS 130 basic) with 160rpm for 20min to refresh the yeast cells. During this period, carbon dioxide (CO2) and alcohol were produced and the bubbles were formed. Finally, the yeast cells became activated and started to reproduce. They were then ready for imaging. The photoluminescence intensity of L-CnDs on yeast cells was compared with Hoechst 33,342 (Thermo Fisher Scientific), a standard commercial dye.

Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was used to measure the average intensities of the L-CnDs on yeast cells under the laser excitation wavelength of 405nm. The CLSM quantum yield of L-CnDs was evaluated using Hoechst 33,342 dye as a reference. The equation for calculating quantum yield is given by [35],
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
I S
f R
n S 2
I R
f S
n R 2
where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the averaged intensities, ƒ=1–10 -Abs is the absorbance factors (Abs is absorbance at the excitation wavelength of 405nm). The subscripts S and R denote the sample and the reference, respectively. The absorbance is typically less than 0.1 in order to avoid the scattering loss at the interfaces and contaminants in solvent. n is the refractive index of the solvent. Herein, distilled water with the refractive index of 1.33 was used for dissolving both L-CnDs and Hoechst 33342.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical, chemical and optical properties of L-CnDs
ce:section.ce:para: As seen from the HRTEM images, the planar sizes of the L-CnDs were in the range of 2–12nm with an average diameter of ~5nm (Fig. 1(b)). The average thickness was ~7nm (Fig. S3 in SI). Highly crystalline structure of L-CnDs was showed in Fig. 1(c). The lattice spacing of 0.21nm is corresponded to the d(1100) plane indicating the distance between two neighbors of carbon atoms on the armchair sites of graphene [31]. The lattice spacing of 0.24nm is the d(1120) plane in the sp2 clusters as observed in GQDs [13,15]. This distance may refer to the zigzag orientation in GQDs. The separation distance of 0.14nm on the right frame is the distance between the nearest neighbors of carbon sublattices in the graphene [14]. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the selected area diffraction (inset in Fig. 1(c)) presented a hexagonal pattern according to the carbon lattices in graphene. The HRTEM results were in agreement with the Raman spectrum of L-CnDs (Fig. 2(a)). Similar to graphene and other low-dimensional carbon materials, the D-band (1367cm−1) and G-band (1580cm−1) indicate the defects in the sp2-hybridized carbon structure and the stretching of CC bond, respectively. The D-band to G-band intensity ratio (ID/IG) of L-CnDs was about 0.70 indicating the disordered fraction of sp2 structure similar to that of graphene oxide [31,36].

FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2(b) indicated that L-CnDs are passivated by the functional groups. The structures are similar to those of graphene oxide and graphene oxide nanodot [9,16,36]. The functional groups form during the carbonization process of acids in limeade which mimic the pyrolysis process of citric acid [16]. The transmittance peaks at 1701, 1623, 1375, 1164 and 1024cm−1 indicate the CO, CN [32] and CC, COH, CO, and COC functional groups, respectively. The tiny peak at 1440cm−1 also indicates the existence of CN functional group [32]. Highly aliphatic compounds such as aldehyde also contribute to the CH stretching vibration and the CH bending at 2930cm−1, and at 918 and 750cm−1, respectively [16]. The broad feature in high wavenumbers (3200–3500cm−1) may refer to the superimposition in the stretching bands of amide NH and hydroxyl OH functional groups.

The deconvolutions of the peaks in the high resolution XPS revealed the nature of the 1s-core electrons in L-CnDs (Fig. 2(c)). Three main elements relating to the optical properties of L-CnDs were investigated, including carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. After fitted by Gaussian function, the C1s, O1s and N1s spectra provided information about the binding energies between the radicals in the functional groups and the carbon atoms. These functional groups build up the surface states and enhance the photoluminescence of L-CnDs. In the C1s, the deconvoluted peaks at 284.0, 285.1, 287.3 and 288.3eV are attributed to the CC bond, CC/CH bonds, CO bond, and CO/CN bonds, respectively [25,32]. Two deconvoluted peaks at 531.3 and 532.5eV in the O1s spectrum are assigned to the CO and CO bonds, respectively. The peaks in the N1s spectrum are attributed to the in CNC (at 399.5eV) and NH (at 399.8eV) bonds [3,25]. The results were consistent with those from EDX elemental mappings in Fig. 2(d). L-CnDs contained 67.43at.% carbon, 27.91at.% oxygen and 4.10at.% nitrogen. The atomic weight ratio of carbon to oxygen of 2.41 was similar to that of the thermal treatment of graphene oxide [37].

The absorption spectrum of L-CnD solution (black line, Fig. 3(a)) showed a broadening of the UV peak from 200 to 450nm. The UV band around 230nm is ascribed to the π→π* transition in the conjugated system of the CC bonds. The shoulder at around 300nm is due to the n→π* transition in the double bonded CO radical. The presence of two absorption bands are the same as those observed in the carbon quantum dots [9,16,25,36,38]. In this case, one possible explanation of the photoluminescence of L-CnDs may refer to the energy difference between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in the L-CnDs [39]. The HOMO level can be promoted by the presence of one electron pair on a non-bonding component (n) and on an aromatic π (π) that hence leads to the energy transfer for n→π* and π→π* transitions, respectively. Absorption of longer wavelength may be possible in the L-CnDs, i.e. 400–550nm. This allows for the creation of localized radiative recombination e–h pairs and then the photoluminescence releases during the de-excitation process [7,8,13,39]. The photoluminescence spectrum of L-CnD solution (blue line, Fig. 3(a)) with the peak at 490nm was excited by the laser beam with the wavelength of 325nm at room temperature. The broadening of the photoluminescence band, e.g. 375–750nm, may be due to the disorder induced by defects and functional groups on the surfaces and edges of the nano-sized sheets of L-CnDs [19]. For example, the coupling of the radicals in such functional groups, e.g. the carbonyl and amide groups, with the carbons can alter the energy gap, the photoluminescence, and the quantum yield of the L-CnDs [28–32]. In addition, Fig. 3(b) to (e) revealed the CLSM emission images and emission spectra of the micron-sized flake of as-prepared L-CnD powder. Here, the surface of the flake may be covered by a number of L-CnDs. The flake displayed multicolored photoluminescence in a range from light-green to red depending on the excitation wavelengths, whereas the photoluminescent intensities upon exposure to different excitation wavelengths were shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: L-CnDs as a probe for rapid detection of ferric ions in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: L-CnDs were capable of being a probe for rapid detection of Fe3+ ions. The difference in the photoluminescence quenching of L-CnD solution in the presence of Fe3+ ions could be distinguished by naked eyes (inset in Fig. 4(a)) and the changes in their normalized absorption spectra were in the main part of Fig. 4(a). The absorbance of L-CnD solution in the presence of Fe3+ ions (red line) showed the strongest absorption in the UV region (200–350nm) among other metal ions. Quenching mechanism of L-CnDs and Fe3+ ions interaction may be related to the oxygen-containing functional groups, e.g. CO, C–OH and especially the most forming of CO from citric acid in limeade, on surface and edges of L-CnDs. The lone-pair electrons in such functional groups are able to interact with Fe3+ ions and quench the photoluminescence of L-CnDs (Fig. 4(b)). These phenomena contribute to the detection behavior of L-CnDs due to Fe3+ ions. Indeed, the radicals in the functional groups may have already transferred the charges to the 3d-orbital of Fe3+ electronic structure. They then exhibit as the oxidants in the reduction reaction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, as found in the Fenton reaction (Fe3+ +e− →Fe2+) [3,19]. The transfer of electrons to the Fe3+ ions instead of undergoing an e–h recombination may be another possible reason for photoluminescence quenching of L-CnDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions [15,20,21,36,38–42]. The high selectivity of L-CnDs to Fe3+ ions may due to the high charge-to-radius ratio, short-range ionic radius and unique coordinate interaction of Fe3+ ions [41]. Among them, the coordinate interaction between Fe3+ ions and the phenolic hydroxyl group on the L-CnDs may play an important role on the selectivity. [43,44] The stretching vibration of the phenolic hydroxyl group (COH) at 1375cm−1 was presented in the FTIR results (Fig. 2(b)). The coordinate interaction could lead to the formation of the complex structure. Shining UV light through the complex structure of ferric ions and L-CnDs may induce the charge transfer from the L-CnDs to ferric ions (Fig. 4(b)), disturb the electronic state of L-CnDs and then quench the fluorescence intensity of L-CnDs [38].

Further investigation was carried on with Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions, each with the concentration of 1.67mM. L-CnDs solution with Fe3+ ions showed the highest quenching, whereas one with Cu2+ ions presented little photoluminescence quenching as observed by naked eyes. Other metal ions did not show photoluminescent reduction (see Fig. S4). The bar graph in Fig. 4(c) presented the detection efficiency of the L-CnDs in various metal ions and the quantitative results were analyzed by the absorbance ratio (A/A0 ratio). For A=A0, the A/A0 ratio is 1, implying no change in the absorbance values and, hence, no photoluminescence quenching. The higher the A/A0 ratio, the better the detection efficiency of the L-CnDs is. From the graph, the A/A0 ratio of L-CnDs in the presence of Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Ni2+ ions were 10.22, 1.52, 1.18, 0.85, 0.89, 9.93 and 0.85 respectively. Here, the highest efficiency was obtained from L-CnDs solution in the presence of Fe3+ ions.

To gain more information of using L-CnDs for detecting Fe3+ ions, Fe3+ ions of different concentrations, including 0.020, 0.099, 0.196, 0.385, 0.566, 0.740 or 0.909mM, were used. Changes in the absorbance values can be observed in Fig. 5(a). The A/A0 ratios were linearly dependent on the concentration of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5(b)). The lowest concentration that can be analyzed by UV–Vis spectroscopy was 0.020mM with the A/A0 ratio of 1.86. The lowest concentration of Fe3+ ions that can be observed by naked eyes were 0.099mM (26.75mg/L) as seen in Fig. 5(c). The technique may be useful for fast screening of iron overload in drinking water and in the open water. [45].

In this section, L-CnDs were demonstrated as imaging agents for imaging living yeast cells using the confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The photoluminescent image of yeast cells in Fig. 6(a) presented the boundary, shape and size of the yeast cell clearly. The regular (A1) and irregular (D1 or D2) shapes of living yeast cells could be determined easily. The reproduction of the yeast cells during imaging were also observed, including budding (A2) and separation of parent and daughter cells (A3). This observation was in agreement with earlier studies that carbon-based quantum dots were harmless to living cells [5,6]. In addition, a cytotoxicity of L-CnDs was carried out at different L-CnD concentrations ranging from 0.0001 to 10mg/ml. The normal HK-2 cells were examined via a MTT assay as shown in Fig. S7. The non-cytotoxicity was observed after incubation for 24h even at high concentration of 10.0mg/ml which is much higher than previous reports [6,9,27]. Fig. 6(b) showed the average intensity as a function of time. The average intensity was obtained from the fluorescence intensity on the yeast cells (see Fig. S5 in SI). After mixing with the yeast cells, L-CnDs need a time period of 20min to reach the yeast cells according to the diffusion process. So, the average intensity of the yeast cells is low at the beginning of the experiment and becomes stable after 20min which is the recommended time for cell imaging. The photoluminescence intensities of L-CnDs on the yeast cells were stable and retained the photoluminescence for more than an hour under continuous observation, whereas those of the Hoechst 33342 dye on the cells decreased and faded out after 15-min observation (Fig. S6). In addition, the regions used for measuring the L-CnD intensities were presented in Fig. S5. The interactions between L-CnDs and yeast cell may be mainly due to the charges of the L-CnDs and those of the cellular surface. The internalization of L-CnDs into the yeast cells via endocytosis is mentioned here because of their few-nanometer size L-CnDs and surface chemistry [40]. The evidence is presented in Fig. 6(c) for the top-view image (x–y plane) of the yeast cells with L-CnDs. Their depth images along z-axis direction were corresponded to the vertical and horizontal planes in the figure. The CLSM quantum yield of the L-CnDs solution (pH7) was about 53.6% using Hoechst 33,342 as a reference when bounded in yeast cells with the same conditions (see parameter details in Table S2 in SI). The quantum yield of L-CnD solution (no the yeast cells) was also investigated by the photoluminescence spectroscopy. The methods were in the supplementary information. The quantum yield from the photoluminescence spectroscopy was 50% which is similar to the CLSM quantum yield (see Table S3 in SI). The slightly different values of the quantum yield may be due to the different standard reference that leads to the difference of excitation and emission wavelengths. The value was higher than those reported in the earlier studies as seen in Fig. 6(d). The high quantum yield of the L-CnDs may be due to the high content of the surface passivation from of lime acid and nitrogen functional groups, COOH, OH, CN and NH as shown in the FTIR spectrum and XPS analysis (Fig. 2) [25–30]. However, to gain more information of the photoluminescence behavior the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) technique might be proposed to study in the future. [46] The possibility of using L-CnDs for real-time monitoring of changes in cell morphology, e.g. size and shape, may be important for medical applications [5,6,40].

The cost-effective and eco-friendly method to prepare photoluminescence carbon nanodots in the gram scale has been reported. The micron-sized flake of the as-prepared L-CnD powder exhibits the excitation-wavelength dependent photoluminescence. The CLSM quantum yield of L-CnDs is about 53.6% when bound in living yeast cells without any external chemical doping. The PL quantum yield of L-CnD solution (no the yeast cells used) is about 50% which is similar to the CLSM quantum yield. The L-CnDs present fast photoluminescence quenching for detecting Fe3+ ions contaminated in water. The lowest concentrations that can be observed by naked eyes and by UV–Vis spectroscopy are 100 and 20μM, respectively. The L-CnDs may be useful for rapid screening of iron overload found in environment, drinking water and medical diagnosis. The L-CnDs have also been used as bioimaging agents to image the morphology of living yeast cells. High photostability and biocompatibility of L-CnDs offer a good chance for continuous monitoring changes in structure, shape and size of the cells upon exposure to certain diseases. In addition, L-CnDs are capable to combine easily with polymers to provide fluorescent polymer composites which may be useful for biosensing and light-emitting applications (see Fig. S9).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by ‘A New Researcher Scholarship of CSTS, MOST: SCH-NR2012-205’ provided by Coordinating Center for Thai Government Science and Technology Scholarship Students (CSTS), National Science and Technology Development Agency) – NSTDA, Thailand. We also thank Mr. Worapot Prongmanee for assistance of EDX mapping. We gratefully acknowledge Center of Nanoimaging for technical assistance of CLSM imaging and Excellent Center for Drug Discovery (ECDD) for providing cell viability via MTT assay at Faculty of Science, Mahidol University. In addition, PS appreciatively acknowledges the Development and Promotion of Science and Technology (DPST) Program for support scholarship Ph.D. Research program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2016.07.075.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), National nutrient database for standard reference release 28: Basic Report 09159, Limes (The National Agricultural Research Library).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Environmental Toxicology: Selected Entries from the Encyclopedia of Sustainability Science and Technology
